steps.instructions: Confirm PACS server is online and no downtime or configuration changes have occurred.

steps.title: Restart System and Retry Export
steps.instructions: Reboot the system and attempt to resend the study.

CCR.Complaint: Ultrasound system unable to send studies to PACS; exams not appearing in imaging system.
CCR.Cause: Incorrect DICOM AE Title configuration preventing successful association with PACS server.
CCR.Resolution: Corrected DICOM configuration settings and verified successful export using echo test and study transmission.

title: GE Healthcare Vivid Series Ultrasound System - Touchscreen or Control Panel Unresponsive
description: Troubleshooting unresponsive touchscreen or control panel due to freezes, input failure, connection issues, or external hardware faults.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the system is in use, safely stop the exam and move the patient to another ultrasound system.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Attempt to use the touchscreen and control panel and determine whether the entire screen is unresponsive, only certain areas/buttons are not working, or if the trackball or keyboard still responds.

steps.title: Check for System Freeze or Software Lockup
steps.instructions: Observe for frozen image, no screen updates, or no response to inputs and attempt a soft reset or reboot using the power button.

steps.title: Inspect External Peripherals and Input Devices
steps.instructions: Check trackball movement, keyboard response, and touchscreen condition and clean the screen if contaminated.

steps.title: Check for Physical Damage or Obstructions
steps.instructions: Inspect for cracked touchscreen, stuck buttons, or signs of liquid intrusion and test for inconsistent response across the screen.

steps.title: Verify System Connections (If Accessible Externally)
steps.instructions: Ensure any external cables or modules related to the control panel are secure without disassembly.

steps.title: Check for Overheating or Environmental Issues
steps.instructions: Check for excessive heat and ensure vents are not blocked.

steps.title: Perform Full System Restart (Power Cycle)
steps.instructions: Shut down the system completely, disconnect from power briefly if appropriate, then restart.

CCR.Complaint: Ultrasound system touchscreen and control panel not responding during exam.
CCR.Cause: System software lockup causing loss of input functionality.
CCR.Resolution: Performed system reboot, restoring normal touchscreen and control panel operation.

title: GE Healthcare CARESCAPE B850 Patient Monitor - Error: 0xHOST1001 Internal temperature out of spec
description: Error indicating internal overheating leading to shutdowns or unstable operation due to temperature outside safe limits.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove device from patient use and transition to backup monitoring equipment.

steps.title: Verify Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm error presence, note timing, and observe symptoms like heat or shutdown.

steps.title: Allow Device to Cool Down
steps.instructions: Power off, disconnect, and allow unit to rest 15–30 minutes.

steps.title: Check Environmental Conditions
steps.instructions: Ensure proper room temperature, airflow, and no nearby heat sources.

steps.title: Inspect Airflow and Ventilation
steps.instructions: Clear vents, ensure spacing, and confirm fan operation.

steps.title: Inspect Physical Condition
steps.instructions: Check for dust, contamination, or abnormal heat and noise.

steps.title: Use Diagnostics
steps.instructions: Review system temperature data and hardware stats if available.

CCR.Complaint: Monitor displaying Error 0xHOST1001 with overheating and intermittent shutdown.
CCR.Cause: Internal temperature out of spec with environmental causes ruled out, suggesting cooling or sensor failure.
CCR.Resolution: Device cooled, airflow verified, error persisted, unit removed from service and sent for repair.

title: GE Healthcare CARESCAPE B850 Patient Monitor - Error: 0xHOST1002 Internal voltage out of spec
description: Error 0xHOST1002 indicating internal voltage out of specification causing instability, shutdowns, or unreliable monitoring

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove monitor from patient use and transfer to backup device

steps.title: Verify Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm error presence and observe when it occurs and associated symptoms

steps.title: Check External Power Source
steps.instructions: Verify outlet functionality and stable AC supply

steps.title: Inspect Power Cord
steps.instructions: Check for damage and reseat or replace cord

steps.title: Check Battery
steps.instructions: Reseat or remove battery and inspect condition

steps.title: Review Voltage via Webmin
steps.instructions: Check internal voltage rails for abnormal values

steps.title: Power Cycle
steps.instructions: Restart device after full power removal

steps.title: Isolate Environment
steps.instructions: Move device to different outlet or circuit

CCR.Complaint: Monitor displaying Error 0xHOST1002 with intermittent rebooting
CCR.Cause: Internal voltage out of spec with external power verified
CCR.Resolution: Removed from service and sent for repair

title: GE Healthcare CARESCAPE B850 Patient Monitor - Error: 0xHOST1004 Disk usage > 90%
description: Error indicating internal storage usage exceeds 90%, leading to slow performance, inability to store data, and potential system instability.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the monitor from patient use and transfer monitoring to a backup device.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm Error 0xHOST1004 is displayed and observe when it occurs and associated symptoms.

steps.title: Check for Excess Stored Data
steps.instructions: Review stored patient data, trends, and logs to identify excessive storage usage.

steps.title: Back Up Required Data
steps.instructions: Export necessary patient data to external storage before deleting any files.

steps.title: Clear Unnecessary Data
steps.instructions: Delete old records, logs, and unused files to reduce storage usage.

steps.title: Reboot the Monitor
steps.instructions: Power cycle the device and verify the error is resolved.

steps.title: Determine if Issue is Persistent
steps.instructions: Monitor storage levels and check if disk usage rapidly increases again.

CCR.Complaint: Monitor displaying Error 0xHOST1004 with slow performance and inability to save patient data.
CCR.Cause: Internal storage exceeded 90% due to accumulated patient data and system logs.
CCR.Resolution: Backed up data, cleared storage, and rebooted device. Escalated for software reinstall if issue persisted.

title: GE Healthcare CARESCAPE B850 Patient Monitor - Error: 0xHOST1100 Timekeeper Battery Depleted
description: Error 0xHOST1100 indicates a depleted timekeeper battery causing incorrect time/date, loss of memory, and potential EMR communication issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove monitor from patient use and transfer to backup device if needed.

steps.title: Verify Error
steps.instructions: Confirm Error 0xHOST1100 and check time/date accuracy.

steps.title: Reset Time and Date
steps.instructions: Manually set correct time/date and power cycle the device.

steps.title: Power Cycle and Recheck
steps.instructions: Turn off, disconnect power, restart, and verify if time resets.

steps.title: Inspect External Factors
steps.instructions: Verify stable power and rule out recent outages or configuration changes.

steps.title: Replace Timekeeper Battery
steps.instructions: Replace internal battery per policy and power on device.

steps.title: Reconfigure System
steps.instructions: Verify network and EMR communication settings after battery replacement.

steps.title: Verify Resolution
steps.instructions: Ensure time/date holds and error does not return.

CCR.Complaint: Monitor displaying Error 0xHOST1100 with incorrect time and date.
CCR.Cause: Internal CPU timekeeper battery depleted and unable to retain system memory.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced battery, reset time/date, and verified proper retention and system communication.

title: GE Healthcare CARESCAPE B850 Patient Monitor - Alarm: Service Monitor Activation Failed
description: Service Monitor Activation Failed alarm preventing activation of software features or settings, limiting monitor functionality.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the monitor from patient use if functionality is affected and transition to a backup device.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the alarm message and test system features to identify activation failure.

steps.title: Perform a Full Power Cycle
steps.instructions: Power off, disconnect AC power, wait, and restart the monitor.

steps.title: Check Network Connectivity
steps.instructions: Ensure Ethernet connection is secure and network configuration is valid.

steps.title: Verify User Permissions
steps.instructions: Log in with appropriate credentials and retry activation.

steps.title: Check Configuration Settings
steps.instructions: Review and reset recent configuration changes if needed.

steps.title: Restart and Observe for Repeat Failure
steps.instructions: Restart the monitor and confirm if the alarm persists.

CCR.Complaint: Service Monitor Activation Failed alarm displayed; unable to activate system settings.
CCR.Cause: Activation failure persisted after power cycle and configuration checks, indicating internal software or licensing issue.
CCR.Resolution: Removed from service, labeled out of service, and sent for software reinstallation and reactivation.

title: GE Healthcare CARESCAPE B850 Patient Monitor - Alarm: External alarm light disconnect
description: Alarm indicating loss of communication with the external alarm light, typically due to USB disconnection or display standby, resulting in loss of remote alarm visibility.

steps.title: Verify Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm alarm message and observe external alarm light function and display status.

steps.title: Check USB Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect and reseat USB cable between CPU and display; ensure secure connection.

steps.title: Check Display Power
steps.instructions: Ensure display is powered on and not in standby mode.

steps.title: Confirm Configuration
steps.instructions: Determine if disconnection or display state is intentional.

steps.title: Acknowledge Alarm
steps.instructions: Silence alarm if condition is intentional using Audio Pause.

steps.title: Evaluate Persistent Issue
steps.instructions: Test with known-good cable and inspect ports for damage.

CCR.Complaint: Monitor displaying external alarm light disconnect alarm with no remote alarm indication.
CCR.Cause: Loose USB connection or internal communication/interface board failure.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated cable and restored function or removed from service and sent for repair.

title: GE Healthcare CARESCAPE B850 Patient Monitor - Alarm: Module Voltage Low
description: Alarm indicating low module voltage causing loss of parameters or unstable monitoring due to power or internal regulation issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove from patient use if unreliable and transition to backup monitoring.

steps.title: Verify the Reported alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm alarm message and note when it occurs and affected modules.

steps.title: Check Power Source and External Supply
steps.instructions: Verify outlet, remove power strips, and ensure stable AC input.

steps.title: Inspect Power Cord and AC Input Connection
steps.instructions: Check for damage, loose connections, and intermittent power.

steps.title: Check Battery Condition and Operation
steps.instructions: Test battery function and observe charging behavior.

steps.title: Inspect Module Seating and Connections
steps.instructions: Reseat modules and inspect for debris or bent pins.

steps.title: Remove Non-Essential Modules to Isolate Issue
steps.instructions: Add modules one at a time to identify faulty component.

steps.title: Perform Power Cycle
steps.instructions: Fully power down, disconnect power, and restart device.

CCR.Complaint: Monitor displaying 'Module Voltage Low' error with intermittent parameter loss.
CCR.Cause: Likely internal voltage regulation issue or faulty module after external causes ruled out.
CCR.Resolution: Removed from service and sent for repair and internal evaluation.

title: GE Healthcare CARESCAPE B850 Patient Monitor - Alarm: Speaker Failure
description: Speaker Failure alarm resulting in no audible alarms, preventing proper patient monitoring notification and alarm audibility.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove monitor from patient use and transfer to a functioning unit with audible alarms.

steps.title: Verify Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm Speaker Failure alarm and test for absence of sound output.

steps.title: Check Alarm Settings
steps.instructions: Ensure volume is not muted and is set to maximum.

steps.title: Inspect External Connections
steps.instructions: Check and reseat any external speaker cables or audio connections.

steps.title: Perform Power Cycle
steps.instructions: Restart monitor to clear temporary faults.

steps.title: Test Audio Output
steps.instructions: Trigger alarms and verify sound output returns.

steps.title: Isolate Likely Cause
steps.instructions: Determine if issue is settings, cable, speaker, or internal audio hardware failure.

CCR.Complaint: Monitor displaying Speaker Failure alarm with no audible alarm tones.
CCR.Cause: No audio output after verifying settings, connections, and performing power cycle, indicating likely internal speaker failure.
CCR.Resolution: Removed from service and sent for repair after unsuccessful external troubleshooting.

title: GE Healthcare CARESCAPE B650 Patient Monitor - Error: 0xHOST1001 Internal temperature out of specification
description: Error 0xHOST1001 indicates internal temperature out of range, leading to overheating, shutdowns, or unstable monitoring performance.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the monitor from patient use if overheating or shutting down. Transfer patient to a backup monitor.

steps.instructions: Confirm Error 0xHOST1001 appears and observe when it occurs along with heat or shutdown symptoms.

steps.title: Allow Device to Stabilize
steps.instructions: Power off, disconnect, and allow the monitor to sit 20–30 minutes before restarting.

steps.title: Check Airflow and Ventilation
steps.instructions: Inspect vents, remove obstructions, and ensure proper airflow around the device.

steps.title: Verify Environmental Conditions
steps.instructions: Ensure normal room temperature and keep device away from heat sources or enclosed spaces.

steps.title: Check Cooling Function
steps.instructions: Listen for fan operation and observe for excessive heat or repeated shutdowns.

steps.title: Use Diagnostics
steps.instructions: Access hardware stats via Webmin and review temperature sensor readings if available.

CCR.Complaint: Monitor displaying Error 0xHOST1001 with intermittent shutdown and excessive heat.
CCR.Cause: Internal temperature out of specification with airflow and environmental causes ruled out.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned vents, verified environment, allowed cooling. Error persisted and device sent for repair.

title: GE Healthcare CARESCAPE B650 Patient Monitor - Error: 0xHOST1002 Internal voltage out of specification
description: Error 0xHOST1002 indicating internal voltage instability that can cause shutdowns, alarms, or unreliable monitoring performance.

steps.instructions: Confirm Error 0xHOST1002 and observe behavior and timing.

steps.title: Inspect Power Cord
steps.instructions: Check and swap power cord if needed.

steps.title: Power Cycle

steps.title: Check Environment
steps.instructions: Ensure proper airflow and temperature.

steps.title: Check Webmin Hardware Stats
steps.instructions: Identify voltage rails out of specification.

title: GE Healthcare CARESCAPE B650 Patient Monitor - Error: 0xHOST1102 User Interface Board Communication Failure

title: GE Healthcare CARESCAPE B650 Patient Monitor - Error: 0xHOST1100 CPU battery needs to be replaced
description: Error 0xHOST1100 indicating depleted CPU battery causing time/date loss, potential data issues, and system instability.

steps.title: Check System Time and Date

description: Service Monitor – Activation Failed alarm preventing software or license activation and limiting monitor functionality.

steps.title: Evaluate Audio Output

title: GE Healthcare Revolution Series CT Scanner - Gantry Rotation Fault or Abnormal Noise
description: Troubleshooting guide for gantry rotation faults, abnormal grinding, rubbing, clicking, vibration, or loud mechanical noise during CT gantry movement on a GE Healthcare Revolution Series CT Scanner.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Stop the scan immediately if abnormal gantry noise, vibration, rotation fault, or unexpected movement occurs. Remove the patient from the scanner if safe to do so. Do not continue scanning if the gantry noise is severe or worsening. Notify CT staff that the scanner should not be used until evaluated. Place the system out of service if rotation faults repeat. Expected: Patient is removed safely and the scanner is prevented from causing injury or equipment damage. Why it matters: Gantry rotation involves high-speed mechanical movement. A bearing, drive, brake, encoder, cable, or obstruction issue can worsen quickly if scanning continues.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Fault and Noise
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact symptom reported by CT staff. Ask when the issue occurs: during startup, during gantry reset, during scout/localizer, during axial scan, during helical scan, only at certain gantry angles, or only at high-speed rotation. Record any displayed error message, fault code, or system event. Ask whether the noise is new, intermittent, or progressively worse. Determine if image acquisition stopped or if the scan completed. Expected: The failure condition is clearly documented before service troubleshooting begins. Why it matters: A fault during high-speed rotation points more toward the drive, bearing, encoder, slip ring, or gantry control system. Noise during slow movement may point more toward obstruction, cover interference, brake drag, or mechanical rubbing.

steps.title: Listen for the Type of Abnormal Noise
steps.instructions: Different sounds can point toward different failure areas. With the system idle and safe, ask staff to describe the sound: grinding, scraping, rubbing, clicking, thumping, high-pitched squeal, heavy vibration, uneven rotation sound, or metal-on-metal noise. Expected: The noise description helps narrow down the likely mechanical area. Possible meaning: Grinding may indicate bearing, roller, brake, or rotating assembly issue. Scraping/rubbing may indicate cover interference, loose component, cable drag, or debris. Clicking/thumping may indicate loose hardware, gantry imbalance, mechanical impact, or drive issue. Squealing may indicate belt, bearing, brake, or motor-related issue. Vibration may indicate imbalance, bearing wear, drive instability, or rotor issue.

steps.title: Check for External Obstruction Around the Gantry
steps.instructions: Look for anything interfering with gantry movement. Inspect around the gantry opening. Check for linens, straps, positioning aids, IV lines, ECG leads, oxygen tubing, or patient accessories. Verify nothing is caught between the table, gantry, or bore area. Inspect visible covers for cracks, displacement, or signs of rubbing. Check if any trim panels appear loose or shifted. Expected: No external objects or covers are interfering with gantry rotation. Why it matters: A simple obstruction or displaced cover can create abnormal noise and may trigger a rotation fault.

steps.title: Check Table and Gantry Clearance
steps.instructions: A table-positioning or patient setup issue can sometimes be mistaken for gantry noise. Inspect the patient table position. Verify the table is not contacting the gantry covers. Check for unusual resistance, collision marks, or rubbing near the gantry opening. Move the table only if safe and permitted by site procedure. Ask whether the fault occurred during table movement, gantry movement, or scanning. Expected: Table movement is normal and there is no contact with the gantry. Why it matters: CT faults may be reported as “gantry noise” when the actual issue is table-to-gantry interference or accessory contact.

steps.title: Inspect Gantry Covers and Visible Mechanical Areas
steps.instructions: Loose covers can cause scraping, rattling, or vibration. Visually inspect the front and rear gantry covers. Check for loose fasteners, cracked panels, missing screws, or shifted covers. Look for scrape marks, plastic dust, metal shavings, or impact marks. Do not remove covers unless authorized and trained. Expected: Covers are secure and there are no signs of rubbing or mechanical contact. Why it matters: Even minor cover misalignment can make loud noise during rotation and may trigger protective faults.

steps.title: Review System Error Messages and Event Logs
steps.instructions: Use the scanner interface or service access to identify the exact fault. Record the displayed error message or code. Check system logs for gantry rotation, rotor, drive, encoder, brake, or speed-related faults. Look for repeated faults around the same time as the reported noise. Note whether the system reports overspeed, underspeed, position error, rotation timeout, servo fault, or gantry control fault. Expected: Logs identify the affected subsystem or provide direction for further service action. Why it matters: Gantry rotation faults are usually controlled by safety monitoring. The system may shut down rotation if position, speed, drive current, or feedback signals are abnormal.

steps.title: Attempt a Controlled Restart Only if Noise Was Minor
steps.instructions: If the issue was a single fault with no severe noise, a controlled restart may clear a temporary control error. Follow facility and manufacturer shutdown procedure. Power down the CT system normally. Wait the required shutdown interval. Restart the system. Observe startup and gantry initialization. Do not run patient scans if abnormal noise returns. Expected: System initializes normally with no abnormal gantry noise or rotation error. Why it matters: Some gantry control faults can be temporary, but repeated faults or mechanical noise should not be treated as a simple reboot issue.

steps.title: Do Not Continue Scanning if Noise Repeats
steps.instructions: Repeated gantry noise should be treated as a mechanical or drive-related failure. Stop use of the scanner. Notify the imaging supervisor. Contact GE service or qualified CT service personnel. Document the fault code, time, scan type, and noise description. Leave the system unavailable for patient use until evaluated. Expected: The scanner remains out of service until the gantry rotation system is checked. Why it matters: Continuing to scan with abnormal gantry noise can damage the rotating assembly, bearing system, drive system, slip ring, or internal cabling.

steps.title: Check for Recent Service, Movement, or Facility Changes
steps.instructions: Recent work can create gantry rotation problems. Ask whether the scanner was recently serviced. Check if covers were recently removed. Ask if the room had power work, HVAC issues, vibration, or construction. Check whether the scanner was recently calibrated, moved, or had tube/detector service. Review whether the issue started after a specific event. Expected: Any recent change is identified and reported to service. Why it matters: Loose covers, disturbed cabling, calibration issues, or mechanical adjustments after service can cause rotation faults or noise.

steps.title: Escalate for Internal Gantry Inspection
steps.instructions: Internal gantry inspection should be performed only by qualified service personnel. Possible areas to inspect: gantry drive motor, drive belt or drive mechanism, gantry bearing system, brake assembly, encoder or position feedback system, slip ring assembly, rotating cable harnesses, loose internal hardware, cooling components, foreign debris inside gantry, rotor balance, or mechanical alignment. Expected: Internal inspection identifies the source of noise or confirms the gantry is safe to operate. Why it matters: Most true gantry rotation faults require trained CT service support because the gantry contains high-voltage, high-speed, and precision-aligned components.

steps.title: Verify System Operation After Repair
steps.instructions: After service correction, confirm safe operation before returning the scanner to clinical use. Run manufacturer-approved system checks. Verify gantry rotation completes normally. Confirm no abnormal noise during startup or test rotation. Run required calibration or QC tests. Confirm no repeat gantry rotation faults in logs. Have CT staff verify normal scan workflow. Expected: Gantry rotation is smooth, quiet, and fault-free. Why it matters: The scanner should not return to patient use until both mechanical operation and system diagnostics are normal.

CCR.Complaint: GE Healthcare Revolution Series CT scanner reported gantry rotation fault with abnormal noise during scan operation.
CCR.Cause: Removed scanner from service. Verified reported noise/fault with CT staff. Inspected external gantry area, table clearance, visible covers, and possible obstructions. Reviewed available system error information. No patient injury reported. External causes were ruled out and gantry rotation system required qualified CT service inspection.
CCR.Resolution: Scanner labeled Out of Service and removed from clinical use pending GE service or qualified CT service evaluation and verification.

helpfulDetails: Exact error message or fault code
Whether fault occurred during startup, scout, axial, or helical scan
Description of alarm behavior or fault behavior
Whether noise is intermittent or constant
Accessories, positioning aids, tubing, leads, or linens checked or swapped
Power behavior during startup, reset, or scan attempt
Environmental factors such as HVAC issues, construction, vibration, or recent room work
Indicator lights, system messages, event logs, or service warnings
Any recent service or room work
Photos of visible damage or loose covers if present
Final device status, including whether the scanner was returned to service or remains Out of Service

title: GE Healthcare Revolution Series CT Scanner - Tube Overheat or Exposure Inhibit Errors
description: Troubleshooting guide for GE Healthcare Revolution Series CT Scanner tube overheat or exposure inhibit errors preventing scanning due to heat load, cooling delay, protocol demand, or tube/generator-related fault.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Stop the scan attempt and confirm the patient is stable. If the patient is on the table, communicate with CT staff before moving the table or restarting any scan sequence. Expected: Patient remains safe and scanning does not continue while the system is inhibiting exposure.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Scan Condition
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact message reported by CT staff, such as tube overheat, exposure inhibit, cooling delay, heat limit, or scan not allowed. Action: Ask what exam or protocol was being performed. Note whether the error occurred before exposure, during scan setup, or after repeated scanning. Check whether the system displays a countdown, cooling status, or tube heat indicator. Expected: Error behavior is confirmed and tied to tube heat/load or exposure prevention.

steps.title: Allow Tube Cooling Time
steps.instructions: Tube heat accumulation is a common cause after high-volume scanning, repeated helical scans, cardiac work, trauma scans, bariatric protocols, or long multiphase studies. Action: Leave the CT system powered on and idle. Allow the tube to cool per system display guidance. Do not repeatedly attempt exposure during the cooling period. Expected: Exposure becomes available again after the tube heat level drops. If the error clears, return the scanner to clinical use and stop.

steps.title: Check for Recent Heavy Workload
steps.instructions: Determine whether the scanner was used heavily before the error occurred. Action: Ask CT staff if multiple high-dose or long scans were performed back-to-back. Review whether the same protocol repeatedly triggers the inhibit. Compare the issue against normal scanner behavior during lower-demand exams. Expected: If the issue only occurs after heavy use, the system may be operating as designed to protect the tube.

steps.title: Review Scan Protocol Demand
steps.instructions: High mA, long scan length, thin slices, multiphase studies, or repeated scout/scan attempts can increase tube heat load. Action: Have CT staff review the selected protocol. Confirm the correct adult, pediatric, body part, or facility-approved protocol is being used. Do not alter clinical protocols yourself unless directed by qualified CT/radiology personnel. Expected: Incorrect or unusually demanding protocol selection is ruled out.

steps.title: Check System Status and Gantry Indicators
steps.instructions: Look for additional system warnings that may point to cooling, generator, gantry, or tube-related issues. Action: Observe console messages. Check gantry status indicators. Listen for abnormal fan, pump, or cooling sounds if accessible externally. Note any unusual smell, excessive heat, or repeated warning behavior. Expected: No additional cooling or hardware warning is present. If abnormal heat, smell, or noise is present, remove the scanner from service and escalate.

steps.title: Verify Room Environment
steps.instructions: CT tube cooling can be affected by room temperature, blocked airflow, or HVAC issues. Action: Confirm CT room temperature feels within normal operating range. Check that scanner vents and gantry airflow paths are not blocked. Look for nearby equipment, linens, carts, or covers restricting ventilation. Notify Facilities if the room is unusually warm or HVAC appears abnormal. Expected: Room conditions and airflow are acceptable.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only If Clinically Appropriate
steps.instructions: If the error appears stuck after adequate cooling time and CT leadership agrees the scanner is not needed for an active patient, perform a controlled system restart according to site practice. Action: Confirm no patient is being scanned. Have CT staff follow normal shutdown/restart workflow. Observe whether the tube heat or exposure inhibit message returns. Expected: Temporary software or status lock clears after restart. If the error clears and system checks pass, return to service per local policy.

steps.title: Check for Repeatability
steps.instructions: Determine whether this was a one-time cooling event or a recurring fault. Action: Ask whether the same error has occurred repeatedly. Note if it happens at low workload, during startup, or with routine exams. Document whether the scanner inhibits exposure even when the tube should be cool. Expected: Recurring errors under normal workload suggest a tube, cooling, generator, sensor, or system control issue requiring service evaluation.

steps.title: Do Not Override Exposure Inhibit
steps.instructions: Exposure inhibit is a protective function. Do not attempt to bypass interlocks, defeat safety controls, or force scanning. Expected: Scanner remains safe and protected from tube or generator damage.

CCR.Complaint: CT staff reported GE Revolution Series scanner displaying tube overheat/exposure inhibit message and preventing scan initiation.
CCR.Cause: Tube heat/load condition did not clear after cooling time and external checks; room airflow and protocol selection were verified with no obvious external cause found.
CCR.Resolution: Removed scanner from service, labeled Out of Service, documented console message and scan conditions, and escalated to imaging service/GE for tube cooling and exposure inhibit evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Exact error or warning message displayed.
Whether the error occurred before, during, or after scanning.
Tube heat/cooling countdown status if shown.
Number of repeated scans or recent heavy workload.
Indicator lights or gantry status messages observed.
Any abnormal fan, pump, gantry, or cooling noise.
Power behavior and whether restart was attempted.

title: GE Healthcare Revolution Series CT Scanner - Detector Calibration Failure
description: Detector calibration failure, image artifact risk, scan inhibit, or calibration abort caused by setup, environmental, or detector-related conditions.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot detector calibration failures during an active patient exam unless the patient is no longer on the table and scanning has stopped. Action: Stop the exam if calibration failure prevents safe imaging. Remove the patient from the scanner room if additional troubleshooting is needed. Use another CT scanner if urgent imaging is required. Expected outcome: Patient care continues safely without repeated failed scan attempts.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Calibration Failure
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact error, when it occurs, and whether it happens during startup, air calibration, daily QC, or a patient scan workflow. Action: Review the operator console message. Note whether the calibration starts and then fails, or never begins. Check whether the system reports detector, DAS, calibration, gantry, tube, or environmental errors. Record the error code or full message if available. Expected outcome: The failure is confirmed and not confused with a separate warm-up, tube, or reconstruction issue.

steps.title: Confirm No Patient, Positioning Aid, Phantom, or Object Is in the Scan Field
steps.instructions: Detector calibration usually requires a clean air path with nothing obstructing the beam. Action: Verify the table is clear. Remove positioning pads, straps, holders, phantoms, contrast tubing, or other objects from the scan field. Confirm the table is positioned as required for calibration. Look through the bore for anything that could block the beam path. Expected outcome: Calibration completes if the failure was caused by an obstruction or incorrect setup. If this resolves the issue, return the scanner to service after successful QC or site-required verification.

steps.title: Check Gantry and Room Conditions
steps.instructions: Detector calibration can fail if the scanner or room environment is outside normal operating conditions. Action: Confirm the room temperature is within the facility’s normal CT operating range. Check for unusual heat, humidity, or recent HVAC issues. Listen for abnormal gantry fan noise. Confirm air vents near the scanner are not blocked. Ask staff whether the room recently had power, HVAC, or temperature problems. Expected outcome: Environmental issues are identified or ruled out. Why it matters: Detector response can drift with temperature changes, and unstable environmental conditions can prevent a valid calibration.

steps.title: Verify System Warm-Up Status
steps.instructions: Some calibration failures occur when the system has not fully stabilized after startup, power loss, or extended idle time. Action: Confirm the scanner completed startup normally. Verify required tube warm-up or system preparation steps were completed. Allow the system to stabilize if it was recently powered on. Repeat the calibration only after the system is ready. Expected outcome: Calibration passes if the failure was caused by an unstable startup or incomplete warm-up condition.

steps.title: Check for Related Tube, Exposure, or High Voltage Errors
steps.instructions: Detector calibration depends on a proper X-ray exposure. A detector calibration failure may actually be caused by exposure inhibit, tube readiness, or generator-related issues. Action: Review the error history for tube, generator, exposure inhibit, rotor, high voltage, or cooling errors. Confirm the system can perform required warm-up exposures. Do not repeatedly force calibration if exposure-related errors are present. Expected outcome: Exposure-related problems are identified before assuming detector failure. Why it matters: The detector cannot calibrate correctly if the system cannot produce a stable exposure.

steps.title: Check Console Workflow and Calibration Selection
steps.instructions: Incorrect calibration type or interrupted workflow can cause failed or incomplete calibration. Action: Confirm the correct calibration or QC routine was selected. Verify the operator did not cancel or interrupt the process. Make sure the scanner is not in a partial exam state. Close unnecessary applications or workflows if the console is sluggish. Restart the application or system only if allowed by site procedure. Expected outcome: Calibration completes if the failure was caused by workflow interruption or incorrect calibration selection.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled System Restart
steps.instructions: A temporary software, console, or communication fault may prevent calibration from completing. Action: Confirm no patient is on the table. Follow the site-approved shutdown and restart process. Allow the scanner to fully boot and stabilize. Retry the required calibration or QC routine. Expected outcome: Calibration completes if the issue was a temporary communication or software state fault.

steps.title: Inspect External Indicators and Listen for Abnormal Operation
steps.instructions: Clinical Engineering should look for signs that the issue may be hardware-related before escalating. Action: Check for abnormal gantry sounds during calibration. Observe for warning indicators on system components, if visible. Note any unusual odor, heat, vibration, or fan noise. Confirm there are no visible cable disconnections, fluid leaks, or physical damage around the scanner, table, or console area. Expected outcome: External signs of hardware failure are documented and the device is not repeatedly operated if unsafe.

steps.title: Review Recent Service, Power, or Room Events
steps.instructions: Detector calibration failures can occur after service activity, power interruptions, room temperature swings, or scanner downtime. Action: Ask staff whether the scanner was recently serviced. Check whether the room had a power outage or breaker event. Confirm whether failures started after software updates, detector service, tube replacement, or gantry work. Document the timing of the first failure. Expected outcome: Recent changes are identified and can be shared with service support.

steps.title: Repeat Calibration Only After Correctable Issues Are Addressed
steps.instructions: Do not repeatedly run failed calibrations without correcting a likely cause. Action: Remove obstructions. Confirm room conditions are stable. Confirm warm-up is complete. Confirm no exposure or gantry errors are active. Retry calibration once or per site policy. Expected outcome: Calibration either completes successfully or fails consistently after external causes are ruled out.

CCR.Complaint: CT staff reported the GE Revolution scanner failed detector calibration during daily QC and would not clear for patient scanning.
CCR.Cause: Clinical Engineering found no patient or object in the bore, room conditions appeared stable, warm-up was completed, and the failure repeated after restart.
CCR.Resolution: Scanner was removed from service, labeled Out of Service, error details were documented, and GE service was contacted for detector calibration failure evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Exact detector calibration error message or code
Whether failure occurred during startup, air calibration, daily QC, or exam setup
Whether the table and bore were clear
Recent power outage or system restart
Recent service, tube replacement, software update, or detector work
Related exposure, generator, DAS, or gantry errors
Abnormal sounds, heat, odor, or fan behavior
Whether calibration passed or failed after restart

title: GE Healthcare Revolution Series CT Scanner - Table Movement or Positioning Errors
description: Table movement, height, cradle, or positioning errors caused by obstructions, controls, accessories, power, or mechanical table issues.

steps.title: 1. Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Stop scanning immediately if table movement is abnormal, delayed, noisy, or uncontrolled. Action: Remove the patient from the table if it is safe to do so. Use proper patient transfer assistance if the table cannot move normally. Do not continue scanning until table motion is verified. Expected outcome: Patient is safe and not dependent on a questionable table movement system.

steps.title: 2. Verify the Reported Table Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm exactly what motion is failing. Action: Identify whether the issue involves table up/down movement. Identify whether the issue involves cradle in/out movement. Identify whether the issue involves table not indexing. Identify whether the issue involves table not moving to scan position. Identify whether the issue involves incorrect displayed table position. Identify whether the issue involves the table stopping mid-travel. Identify whether the issue involves a positioning error at the console. Identify whether the issue involves a gantry/table interlock message. Expected outcome: The failure mode is clearly identified before troubleshooting continues.

steps.title: 3. Check for Physical Obstructions
steps.instructions: External obstructions are a common cause of table movement errors. Action: Inspect around the table base, cradle, gantry opening, floor area, patient accessories, straps, sheets, cables, IV lines, and positioning aids. Verify nothing is caught under the table, between the table and gantry, or along the cradle travel path. Expected outcome: Table path is clear. If an obstruction is found, remove it, retest table movement, and stop if the issue is resolved.

steps.title: 4. Confirm the Emergency Stop Is Not Activated
steps.instructions: A pressed emergency stop can prevent table or gantry motion. Action: Check all accessible emergency stop buttons near the scanner, gantry, operator area, and table area. Reset only if the area is safe and site procedure allows. Expected outcome: Emergency stop circuit is reset and table movement is restored if that was the cause.

steps.title: 5. Check Table Controls at the Gantry
steps.instructions: Determine whether the issue is isolated to one control point. Action: Test table movement using the gantry/table control panel. Check if buttons respond normally. Look for stuck, damaged, or contaminated buttons. Expected outcome: Table responds correctly from local controls. If local controls work but console controls do not, the issue may involve console command, communication, or software control rather than the table drive itself.

steps.title: 6. Check Console Table Commands
steps.instructions: Verify whether the table responds from the operator console. Action: Attempt normal table positioning from the console. Observe if the command is ignored, delayed, or generates an error message. Compare console position display with actual table location. Expected outcome: Console commands and actual table movement agree. If the table moves locally but not from the console, document the difference and escalate for service evaluation.

steps.title: 7. Verify Patient Load and Table Setup
steps.instructions: Excessive weight, uneven loading, or improper setup can affect table movement. Action: Confirm the patient and accessories are within the site-approved table weight limit. Check that the patient is centered and not creating excessive side load. Remove unnecessary accessories or positioning devices if safe. Expected outcome: Table is not overloaded or mechanically restricted.

steps.title: 8. Inspect Table Accessories and Cradle Interface
steps.instructions: Improperly seated accessories may interfere with travel or positioning. Action: Check head holders, pads, extension boards, straps, immobilization devices, and tabletop attachments. Verify accessories are fully seated, locked, and not extending into the gantry or travel path. Expected outcome: Accessories are installed correctly and do not interfere with motion.

steps.title: 9. Listen for Abnormal Mechanical Sounds
steps.instructions: Noise can help separate obstruction issues from mechanical drive problems. Action: With no patient on the table, attempt limited table movement if safe. Listen for grinding, clicking, slipping, binding, or motor strain. Stop testing immediately if movement sounds abnormal. Expected outcome: Table movement is smooth and controlled. If abnormal noise is present, remove the scanner from service and escalate. Do not repeatedly cycle the table.

steps.title: 10. Check Table Position Display and Alignment Behavior
steps.instructions: Positioning errors may occur when the system cannot confirm table location. Action: Observe whether the displayed table position changes when the table moves. Check if the system reports position mismatch, table not ready, or unable to reach scan position. Verify the table returns to expected home or scan position when commanded. Expected outcome: Displayed position matches actual table movement. If actual movement and displayed position do not match, suspect encoder, sensor, calibration, or table control issues requiring service.

steps.title: 11. Perform a Safe System Reset
steps.instructions: A software or communication fault may temporarily affect table positioning. Action: Remove the patient. Follow site-approved CT shutdown and restart procedure. Allow the scanner, console, and table control system to restart fully. Retest table motion without a patient. Expected outcome: Table movement returns to normal if the issue was a temporary control fault. If the issue returns, continue escalation.

steps.title: 12. Verify Environmental or Floor Conditions
steps.instructions: Floor obstruction or table base interference can cause movement problems. Action: Inspect for floor damage, debris, loose covers, fluid, or items near the table base. Confirm nothing is contacting the table pedestal or restricting vertical travel. Expected outcome: Table base and floor area are clean, dry, and unobstructed.

steps.title: 13. Check for Repeated Positioning Error During Scanning
steps.instructions: Determine whether the table only fails during scan setup or indexing. Action: Run only site-approved non-patient movement checks. Observe whether the table reaches scout, scan, or index positions correctly. Note whether the error occurs before scan, during table indexing, or after scan completion. Expected outcome: Error timing is documented clearly for service. If the table cannot reliably position for scanning, do not return the scanner to clinical use.

CCR.Complaint: CT staff reported the GE Revolution CT table would not move to the selected scan position and displayed a positioning error.
CCR.Cause: Physical obstructions and emergency stop status were checked; table movement remained inconsistent with no patient on the table.
CCR.Resolution: Scanner was removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and escalated for service evaluation of the table positioning system.

helpfulDetails: Outlet or room power status checked
Whether movement failed from gantry controls, console controls, or both
Whether the table moved with no patient present
Patient load or accessory setup at time of failure
Unusual noises, binding, vibration, or motor strain
Whether actual table position matched console display
Whether system reset changed the behavior

title: GE Healthcare Revolution Series CT Scanner - Reconstruction or Image Processing Failure
description: Troubleshooting guide for image reconstruction failures, delayed image availability, processing errors, or missing CT images after scan acquisition on a GE Healthcare Revolution Series CT Scanner.

steps.title: 1. Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is no longer dependent on the affected scan session before troubleshooting. Action: Stop additional scanning until image availability is confirmed; Notify CT staff that images may not be available for diagnosis; If urgent imaging is needed, move the patient to another CT scanner if available; Do not repeat radiation exposure until acquisition status and image availability are reviewed. Expected: Patient care continues safely without unnecessary repeat exposure.

steps.title: 2. Verify the Reported Failure
steps.instructions: Confirm exactly what failed. Action: Ask CT staff whether the scan acquired normally; Determine whether the issue is reconstruction failure, delayed reconstruction, frozen processing, missing images, or PACS transfer failure; Check whether raw data is still present; Confirm whether the problem affects one patient, one protocol, or all exams. Expected: The failure type is clearly identified before deeper troubleshooting begins. Why it matters: A PACS transfer issue can look like reconstruction failure, but the troubleshooting path is different.

steps.title: 3. Check the Operator Console Status
steps.instructions: Inspect the CT operator console for visible errors or abnormal behavior. Action: Look for reconstruction queue errors; Check if the console is frozen, slow, or unresponsive; Confirm whether other applications are running normally; Note any displayed error codes or messages exactly as shown. Expected: Console is responsive and reconstruction queue status can be reviewed. If the console is frozen or unresponsive, proceed with safe restart steps only after confirming no active scan or reconstruction is in progress.

steps.title: 4. Confirm the Scan Data Was Acquired
steps.instructions: Make sure the issue is not failed acquisition. Action: Review the completed exam list; Confirm scan series are listed; Check whether scout/localizer and axial/helical data exist; Verify that the correct patient and accession were selected. Expected: Acquired scan data is present and associated with the correct exam. If no scan data exists, the issue may be acquisition-related rather than reconstruction-related.

steps.title: 5. Check Reconstruction Queue or Pending Jobs
steps.instructions: Review whether reconstruction jobs are stuck, paused, failed, or backed up. Action: Open the reconstruction or processing queue; Look for pending, failed, or incomplete recon jobs; Confirm whether multiple studies are waiting to process; Check if one specific series is blocking the queue. Expected: Reconstruction jobs are either actively processing or show a clear failure state. If one job is stuck and others are waiting behind it, document the affected exam and escalate before deleting or altering data.

steps.title: 6. Verify Protocol and Reconstruction Settings
steps.instructions: Confirm that the selected protocol and recon parameters are valid. Action: Review slice thickness, kernel, field of view, recon range, and image series settings; Compare to a known working protocol if available; Check whether the failure occurred after protocol editing; Ask whether this protocol recently changed. Expected: Reconstruction settings are valid and consistent with normal department workflow. Why it matters: Invalid or unusual recon parameters can cause processing errors even when the scan acquisition was successful.

steps.title: 7. Check Storage Capacity
steps.instructions: Low storage can prevent reconstruction or image processing from completing. Action: Check local console storage status if accessible; Review whether the system reports low disk space; Confirm whether older completed exams are being archived properly; Do not delete studies unless following site policy and confirming data is safely archived. Expected: Adequate storage is available for reconstruction and temporary processing. If storage is full or near full, stop scanning and escalate for controlled data management.

steps.title: 8. Check Network and PACS Separation
steps.instructions: Determine whether images failed to reconstruct or simply failed to send. Action: Check whether images are visible locally on the CT console; Confirm whether images are missing only in PACS; Test DICOM send status if available; Check destination status for PACS, archive, or workstation. Expected: Locally reconstructed images are viewable before blaming reconstruction. If images are present locally but absent in PACS, troubleshoot as a DICOM/network transfer issue, not a reconstruction failure.

steps.title: 9. Attempt a Safe Reprocess or Manual Reconstruction
steps.instructions: If raw data is present and the system allows it, attempt reconstruction again using normal operator-level tools. Action: Select the affected raw data or series; Re-run reconstruction using the original or known-good parameters; Monitor whether processing completes; Confirm image series appears locally afterward. Expected: Images reconstruct successfully and become available for review. If successful, verify image transfer to PACS and stop.

steps.title: 10. Restart the Affected Application or Console if Safe
steps.instructions: If reconstruction remains stuck and no active scan is running, a controlled restart may clear a software processing fault. Action: Confirm no patient is being scanned; Confirm no active reconstruction job should be interrupted; Follow site-approved shutdown or restart process; Restart the console or reconstruction application; Recheck the affected exam and reconstruction queue. Expected: Console returns to normal operation and reconstruction can be retried. Do not hard power-cycle CT system components unless directed by service procedure or qualified support.

steps.title: 11. Test With a Non-Patient or Phantom Workflow if Available
steps.instructions: After patient impact is controlled, verify whether the issue is repeatable. Action: Use a site-approved phantom or test workflow if available; Run a simple scan and reconstruction; Confirm local image generation and PACS transfer; Document whether the issue affects all reconstructions or only one exam/protocol. Expected: Test reconstruction completes normally. If test reconstructions fail, the issue is likely system-level.

steps.title: 12. Check for Environmental or System Clues
steps.instructions: Look for signs of workstation or server instability. Action: Listen for abnormal workstation fan noise; Check for excessive heat around console cabinets; Note slow response, freezing, or repeated software errors; Check for recent power events or IT/network downtime. Expected: No obvious external environmental or power-related cause is present. Why it matters: Reconstruction can fail due to workstation performance, storage, network, or server-side processing problems.

CCR.Complaint: CT staff reported that a GE Revolution Series CT exam completed acquisition, but reconstructed images were not available for review.
CCR.Cause: Troubleshooting found the raw scan data present, but the reconstruction queue was stalled and no completed image series was generated.
CCR.Resolution: Confirmed patient was no longer on the scanner, checked local image availability, verified storage and queue status, attempted safe reprocessing, and removed the CT scanner from service for GE imaging service evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Exact reconstruction error message or code
Alarm behavior or displayed processing alerts
Accessories swapped or removed during evaluation
Power behavior, restart behavior, or recent power events
Environmental factors such as heat, console cabinet airflow, or workstation fan noise
Indicator lights, console messages, queue status, or workstation status
Whether images were missing only from PACS
Affected patient exam or accession number, following privacy policy
Whether one protocol or all protocols were affected
Any recent protocol, software, or network changes
Whether console was frozen, slow, or responsive
Whether a restart or reprocess was attempted

title: GE Healthcare AMX Series Mobile X-Ray System - Battery Not Charging or Short Run Time
description: Battery charging problems, reduced drive/run time, weak charge retention, charger connection issues, or battery-related mobile x-ray readiness concerns.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the mobile x-ray unit from clinical use if battery performance may affect imaging, transport, braking, or positioning. Move the patient exam to another available mobile x-ray system if imaging is needed. Do not continue using the AMX unit if it may lose power during transport, positioning, exposure setup, or return to storage. Expected outcome: Patient imaging is not dependent on a mobile x-ray system with unreliable battery operation. Why it matters: A mobile x-ray system with weak or unstable batteries may stop during transport, fail to position correctly, or become unavailable during patient care.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Battery Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the issue is no charging, slow charging, short run time, or sudden power loss. Ask the user when the problem occurs: while plugged into AC power, after being unplugged overnight, during driving, during setup before exposure, after only a few exams, or after sitting unused. Check the battery charge indicator, charging symbol, warning messages, and any audible alarms. Expected outcome: The complaint is narrowed to charging failure, battery capacity loss, user workflow issue, or intermittent power problem. If the issue was caused by misunderstanding the charge indicator or normal low-battery behavior, educate the user, document the finding, and stop.

steps.title: Confirm the Unit Is Connected to a Known-Good AC Outlet
steps.instructions: Check the simplest external charging source first. Verify the AMX power cord is fully plugged into the wall outlet and the unit. Test the outlet with an outlet tester or another approved load. Confirm the outlet is not controlled by a wall switch, emergency circuit behavior, loose receptacle, or failed power strip. Expected outcome: The mobile x-ray system is connected to verified AC power. If the unit begins charging normally on a known-good outlet, return it to service after confirming charge behavior and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the Power Cord and Plug
steps.instructions: Look for damage that could prevent charging or create a safety hazard. Inspect the AC cord, plug blades, strain relief, and cord jacket. Look for cuts, exposed conductors, loose plug fit, burn marks, crushed areas, or signs of overheating. Confirm the cord is seated firmly and not pulling out under light movement. Expected outcome: The power cord is safe and able to supply AC power. If the cord is damaged, remove the unit from service until the cord or cord assembly is repaired or replaced.

steps.title: Check for AC Input or Charging Indicators
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the unit recognizes incoming AC power. Plug the AMX into a verified outlet. Observe the charging indicator, display message, battery icon, AC indicator, or charger status light if present. Listen for abnormal clicking, repeated cycling, fan noise changes, or alarms. Expected outcome: The unit should show some indication that AC power is present and charging is active. If there is no AC recognition on a known-good outlet with a good cord, the issue may involve the charger input path, power supply, charger assembly, or internal connection. Remove from service and send for bench evaluation.

steps.title: Allow Adequate Charging Time
steps.instructions: Rule out incomplete charging before assuming battery failure. Leave the unit plugged into verified AC power for an appropriate charge period based on local practice and device condition. Confirm staff are not repeatedly unplugging the unit before it reaches full charge. Check whether the unit is stored plugged in when not in use. Expected outcome: The battery should recover enough charge for normal operation if the battery and charger are healthy. If the unit charges normally after being left connected, educate the department on charging workflow, document the finding, and stop.

steps.title: Check for High-Load Use Patterns
steps.instructions: Short run time can be caused by heavy use rather than failure. Ask how many exams are performed between charges. Determine whether the unit is used heavily for long transports, repeated positioning, drive motor use, or back-to-back portable exams. Compare run time to another similar AMX unit in the same department if available. Expected outcome: The complaint is separated between abnormal battery performance and expected reduced run time under heavy workload. If run time is similar to other units under the same use pattern, document that no failure was confirmed and recommend normal charging between uses.

steps.title: Inspect Battery Status After Charging
steps.instructions: Check whether the battery reaches and holds charge. After charging, unplug the unit and observe the battery indicator. Drive and position the unit in a safe area away from patients. Watch for rapid charge drop, low-battery warnings, sudden shutdown, weak drive response, or system reboot. Expected outcome: A healthy battery set should hold charge and support normal drive and positioning operation. If the charge drops quickly or the unit shuts down soon after charging, battery capacity loss or battery circuit issues are likely.

steps.title: Check for Environmental or Storage Issues
steps.instructions: Battery life can be affected by storage habits and temperature. Verify the unit is not stored in an unusually hot, cold, or poorly ventilated area. Check whether the unit sits unplugged for extended periods. Ask whether the unit was recently unused, relocated, or stored during construction or room changes. Expected outcome: Storage conditions should support normal battery charging and capacity. If storage or charging workflow caused the issue, correct the workflow, recharge fully, retest, document, and stop.

steps.title: Inspect for Signs of Battery or Charging Hazard
steps.instructions: Do not continue troubleshooting if there are safety warning signs. Check externally for unusual heat, odor, smoke, battery compartment swelling, leaking, corrosion, or charging area discoloration. Do not open battery compartments beyond what is allowed by local Clinical Engineering procedures. Expected outcome: No signs of battery overheating, leakage, or electrical hazard are present. If heat, odor, leakage, swelling, smoke, or burning smell is present, remove the unit from service immediately, label it Out of Service, follow facility battery safety procedures, and escalate for repair.

steps.title: Review Recent Service or Battery Replacement History
steps.instructions: Determine whether the issue follows a recent repair or aging battery set. Check the service history for recent battery replacement, charger repair, drive complaints, blown fuses, charging faults, or repeated low-battery calls. Look for repeated short-run-time complaints over several months. Expected outcome: History helps identify whether this is a new charging issue, aging battery problem, or recurring internal fault. If the battery set is aged, repeatedly failing, or cannot support normal use after full charging, remove from service for battery evaluation or replacement according to facility procedure.

steps.title: Perform a Safe Functional Check After Charging
steps.instructions: Confirm normal operation before returning the unit to service. After charging, power the unit on. Verify normal startup, battery indicator behavior, drive operation, brake release, column/arm movement, and readiness for imaging workflow. Do not perform unnecessary radiation exposure unless required by local test procedure and done safely. Expected outcome: The unit operates normally without low-battery warnings, charging faults, shutdowns, or weak drive behavior. If the AMX passes functional checks and holds charge appropriately, return it to service and document the results.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the GE AMX mobile x-ray unit would not hold a charge and had very short run time after being unplugged.
CCR.Cause: Verified AC outlet and power cord were good, but the battery indicator dropped rapidly during functional testing after charging.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the AMX unit from service, labeled it Out of Service, and sent it for battery and charger bench evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Battery indicator level before and after charging
Run time or drive time observed
Any unusual heat, odor, sound, or visible damage
Environmental factors such as hot, cold, or poorly ventilated storage areas
Recent battery or charger service history

title: GE Healthcare AMX Series Mobile X-Ray System - Unit will not power on or boots intermittently
description: Power-on failure, intermittent booting, charging issues, battery problems, outlet issues, or external power connection problems on GE AMX mobile X-ray units.

steps.title: 1. Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the mobile X-ray unit from clinical use if it will not power on reliably or shuts down during startup. Action: Move imaging needs to another available mobile X-ray system. Do not attempt patient imaging with a unit that may lose power during positioning, exposure setup, or image acquisition. Expected outcome: Patient care continues using a known-working imaging device. Why it matters: A mobile X-ray unit that powers off unexpectedly may delay care, interrupt imaging, or create a positioning hazard near the patient.

steps.title: 2. Verify the Reported Power Issue
steps.instructions: Confirm the actual symptom before assuming a battery or internal power failure. Action: Attempt to power the unit on in a safe area. Observe whether the unit powers on normally. Observe whether the unit does not respond at all. Observe whether the unit starts booting and shuts down. Observe whether the unit turns on only while plugged in. Observe whether the unit displays battery or charging warnings. Observe whether the unit has indicator lights but no screen activity. Expected outcome: The failure mode is clearly identified and documented. If the unit powers on normally and the issue cannot be duplicated, continue with charging and battery checks before returning it to service.

steps.title: 3. Check the Emergency Stop or Power Disable Condition
steps.instructions: Some mobile X-ray systems may appear dead if an emergency stop, transport lock, or power disable condition is active. Action: Inspect the unit for any emergency stop switch, drive disable switch, key switch, or main power control in the wrong position. Reset or return controls to the normal operating position if appropriate. Expected outcome: If the unit was disabled by a switch or safety control, it should power on normally after reset. If this resolves the issue, verify normal startup and document the cause.

steps.title: 4. Verify the AC Outlet
steps.instructions: Rule out a bad wall outlet before troubleshooting the unit. Action: Plug a known-working device or outlet tester into the same outlet. Confirm the outlet has proper power. Avoid using damaged outlets, loose receptacles, extension cords, or power strips unless approved by facility policy. Expected outcome: The outlet is confirmed functional and safe. If the outlet is faulty, notify Facilities and move the unit to a verified outlet.

steps.title: 5. Inspect the Power Cord and Plug
steps.instructions: A damaged power cord can prevent charging or cause intermittent booting. Action: Inspect the AC power cord for cuts, crushed insulation, bent prongs, loose strain relief, burn marks, or signs of overheating. Check that the cord is fully seated at the unit and wall outlet. Gently move the cord while observing charging or power indicators. Expected outcome: The power cord is secure, undamaged, and not causing intermittent power loss. If damage is found, remove the unit from service until the cord or cord assembly is repaired or replaced per facility procedure.

steps.title: 6. Confirm Charging Indicator Behavior
steps.instructions: Determine whether the unit is receiving AC power and attempting to charge. Action: Plug the AMX unit into a verified outlet. Observe charging lights, display messages, fans, relays, or other startup indicators. Allow the unit to remain connected long enough to determine whether the battery begins accepting charge. Expected outcome: The unit should show some indication that AC power is present or charging is active. If there are no indicators at all with a known-good outlet and cord, suspect an internal power input, charger, battery, or power supply issue.

steps.title: 7. Allow Time for a Deeply Discharged Battery
steps.instructions: If the unit has been unplugged for an extended period, the battery may be deeply discharged. Action: Leave the unit connected to a verified AC outlet. After an appropriate charging period, attempt startup again. Watch for changes in battery indicators, charger status, or display behavior. Expected outcome: A deeply discharged unit may recover enough charge to boot after being connected to AC power. If the unit still does not power on or repeatedly fails during startup, continue troubleshooting.

steps.title: 8. Check for Battery-Related Symptoms
steps.instructions: Intermittent booting often points to low battery capacity, poor charging, or battery connection issues. Action: Determine whether the unit only boots while plugged in. Determine whether the unit shuts down when unplugged. Determine whether the unit shows low battery warnings. Determine whether the unit dies during movement. Determine whether the unit has reduced run time. Determine whether the unit fails after sitting unplugged. Determine whether the unit requires repeated startup attempts. Expected outcome: Battery-related behavior is identified. If the unit powers on only with AC power or cannot hold charge, remove it from service for battery and charging system evaluation.

steps.title: 9. Inspect External Battery Compartment Areas Without Disassembly
steps.instructions: Do not perform deep internal disassembly during clinical troubleshooting, but inspect accessible areas for obvious issues. Action: Look for loose covers, missing screws, corrosion, liquid intrusion, impact damage, or signs that the unit was recently dropped or struck. Check for unusual heat, smell, buzzing, clicking, or visible damage near the power entry or battery area. Expected outcome: No obvious external damage, contamination, overheating, or electrical hazard is present. If heat, burning smell, liquid damage, or arcing is suspected, stop troubleshooting and remove the unit from service immediately.

steps.title: 10. Perform a Controlled Restart
steps.instructions: If the unit boots intermittently, perform a clean power cycle in a safe location. Action: Power the unit down fully if possible. Disconnect from AC power briefly. Reconnect to a verified outlet. Attempt one normal startup. Do not repeatedly force startup if the unit continues to fail. Expected outcome: The unit either boots normally or the failure repeats clearly. If the unit starts normally, verify charging status, battery condition, and basic function before considering return to service.

steps.title: 11. Check for Display Versus Power Failure
steps.instructions: A blank screen does not always mean the entire unit has no power. Action: When attempting startup, observe for power indicator lights. When attempting startup, observe for keypad lights. When attempting startup, observe for fans. When attempting startup, observe for drive or brake sounds. When attempting startup, observe for computer boot sounds. When attempting startup, observe for exposure console activity. When attempting startup, observe for error tones or beeps. Expected outcome: You can determine whether the unit is completely dead or powering internally with no display. If indicators are active but the display remains blank, document as a display, computer, or boot/display path issue rather than a total power failure.

steps.title: 12. Review Recent History
steps.instructions: Recent movement, battery replacement, charging complaints, or shutdowns may point to the likely cause. Action: Ask staff when the issue started. Determine whether the unit was recently left unplugged. Determine whether the unit was recently moved between departments. Determine whether the unit was recently involved in a collision. Determine whether the unit was recently cleaned heavily. Determine whether the unit was recently exposed to liquid. Determine whether the unit was recently reported for short battery life. Determine whether the unit was recently serviced or transported. Expected outcome: The work order includes useful context for repair evaluation.

steps.title: 13. Verify Basic Function Only If Startup Succeeds
steps.instructions: If the unit powers on successfully, confirm safe basic operation before returning it to service. Action: Check that the system boots completely. Confirm battery or charging status is acceptable. Verify no active power, battery, drive, brake, generator, or system errors remain. Confirm the unit remains powered on while idle and during basic movement checks. Expected outcome: The unit demonstrates stable startup and no recurring power issue. If the unit boots once but fails again, do not return it to service.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported that the GE AMX mobile X-ray unit would not power on and sometimes began booting before shutting off.
CCR.Cause: Verified outlet and power cord were functional, but the unit showed intermittent startup and unreliable charging behavior.
CCR.Resolution: Removed unit from service, labeled it Out of Service, and sent it for battery, charger, and internal power system evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Whether the unit powered on with AC connected
Whether the unit shut down when unplugged
Any clicking, buzzing, heat, odor, or visible damage

title: GE Healthcare AMX Series Mobile X-Ray System - Exposure Inhibited Due to Generator, Rotor, or Tube Fault
description: Exposure inhibit conditions related to generator readiness, tube heat, rotor preparation, battery condition, or failed exposure sequence.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not continue attempting exposures on a patient if the AMX unit is displaying an exposure inhibit, generator fault, rotor fault, or tube-related error. Remove the mobile X-ray system from patient use. Use another available mobile X-ray unit if imaging is clinically needed. Notify radiology staff that the unit should not be used until evaluated. Expected outcome: Patient imaging continues with a known-ready system. Why it matters: Repeated failed exposure attempts can delay care, increase workflow risk, and may indicate a high-voltage, tube, rotor, or generator readiness problem.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Fault
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact behavior before assuming a major tube or generator failure. Power the unit on in a safe location. Observe the display for fault messages, exposure inhibit messages, warning codes, or status indicators. Ask the technologist when the issue occurs: Before exposure prep. During rotor/prep. At exposure attempt. After movement or transport. Only when battery is low. Only on certain techniques. Expected outcome: The failure point is identified clearly. If the unit now exposes normally, document the condition and perform functional checks before returning it to service.

steps.title: Confirm the Unit Is Not in a Safety Lockout or Inhibit State
steps.instructions: Some exposure inhibit conditions are caused by readiness or safety interlock issues rather than failed internal components. Check whether the unit reports any safety, positioning, cassette/detector, battery, drive, or system-not-ready condition. Confirm the unit is fully booted and not still initializing. Verify the exposure controls are enabled and the system is in the correct imaging mode. Expected outcome: The unit should show ready status before prep or exposure. If a simple inhibit condition clears, perform a test exposure according to site policy and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check AC Power and Charging Status
steps.instructions: Low battery or unstable power can contribute to generator readiness and exposure inhibit problems on mobile X-ray systems. Plug the AMX unit into a known-good hospital-grade outlet. Verify charging indicators, AC connected status, or battery charge display. Allow the unit to charge if the battery level is low. Test the outlet if there is any doubt. Expected outcome: The unit recognizes AC power and battery condition improves. If exposure works after charging, document low battery or insufficient charge as the likely cause.

steps.title: Inspect the Power Cord and Plug
steps.instructions: A damaged power cord can prevent proper charging and cause unreliable exposure readiness. Inspect the cord, plug, strain relief, and cord reel if equipped. Look for cuts, bent blades, loose plug fit, heat discoloration, or intermittent connection. Gently move the cord while observing charging status. Expected outcome: AC input remains stable with no flickering, dropouts, heat, or damage. If the cord or plug is damaged, remove the unit from service and send for repair.

steps.title: Check Battery Condition and Runtime Behavior
steps.instructions: Weak batteries may allow the unit to power on but fail under exposure load. Ask whether the unit loses power, resets, or faults during prep or exposure. Check whether the issue happens more often after the unit has been off charge. Compare battery level before and after exposure attempts. Look for short run time, sudden battery drop, or charge level instability. Expected outcome: Battery level should remain stable enough for normal exposure operation. If battery performance is poor, remove the unit from service for battery and charging system evaluation.

steps.title: Check Exposure Hand Switch or Control Input
steps.instructions: A faulty exposure switch or damaged cable may interrupt the prep/exposure sequence and appear like a rotor or generator issue. Inspect the exposure hand switch, cord, connector, and strain relief. Check for cracked housing, sticky buttons, intermittent operation, or damaged cable jacket. Confirm the prep and expose stages feel normal if using a two-stage switch. If your site has a compatible known-good hand switch, swap it only if approved by local procedure. Expected outcome: Prep and expose commands should operate consistently. If the issue follows the hand switch, replace or repair the accessory and verify operation.

steps.title: Verify Technique Settings Are Reasonable
steps.instructions: Incorrect or extreme technique selections may contribute to exposure inhibit, especially if the tube or generator cannot support the requested exposure. Review selected kVp, mAs, exposure mode, focal spot selection if applicable, and exam preset. Select a basic test technique appropriate for service testing and site policy.
